skill.name: open-skills
skill.description: Interactive CLI for browsing, installing, syncing, and managing AI agent skills across Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, and related editors.
skill.summary: Install and manage AI agent skills across multiple coding editors from one CLI.
skill.author: lumacoder

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits from Markdown backticks, dependency integrity strings, and normal TypeScript imports; those are mostly false positives. Confirmed risks remain because the CLI fetches third-party skill content, writes it into AI editor instruction locations, and exposes a dev panel that can save registry data and render registry-controlled HTML. No evidence found of prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent in the inspected repository files.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Remote Skills Installed Into Agent Instruction Paths
medium_findings.description: The engine clones Git sources from registry metadata and writes resulting SKILL.md content into editor skill or rule paths derived from the selected scope. This is expected for a skill installer, but remote skill instructions can influence AI agents after installation, so users must trust the registry source.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The git clone and file-write flow is directly visible in code, and the target paths are AI editor instruction locations. Risk is moderated because this is core product behavior and requires user-selected installation.

medium_findings.title: Developer API Allows Unauthenticated Registry Writes
medium_findings.description: The development server permits cross-origin requests and accepts POST writes to the registry without authentication. A local browser page could modify registry metadata while the dev server is running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissive CORS headers and saveRegistryV3 call are explicit. Confidence is below very high because the server is launched only in developer mode on localhost.

medium_findings.title: Registry-Controlled Values Rendered With innerHTML
medium_findings.description: The dev panel builds HTML from category and skill fields using innerHTML. Malicious registry values could execute script in the local management UI if they are loaded or saved into registry data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The DOM sinks and registry-derived values are visible in the same render paths. This is a classic XSS pattern, limited to the local developer panel context.

medium_findings.title: Broad Cleanup May Remove Unmanaged Skill Files
medium_findings.description: Before directory-style installs, the engine triggers target directory cleanup, and sync empties the destination before cloning or copying content. This can remove unrelated files under managed skill folders when users install, update, or sync a subset of skills.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The engine cleanup call and sync emptyDir operation are explicit. The exact blast radius depends on target directory contents, so this is a medium operational risk.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is a Development Flag
low_findings.description: The only inspected environment access checks OPEN_SKILLS_DEV to start the local developer server. No evidence found that secrets or credential environment variables are read or exfiltrated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced code compares one named flag to the string 1 and does not read arbitrary environment data. This supports a false-positive verdict for sensitive environment access.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Hits Are Dependency or Metadata Noise
low_findings.description: Several high static hits point to package descriptions, registry metadata, or lockfile integrity strings rather than cryptographic code or command-and-control behavior. No evidence found of encryption, hashing APIs, or beacon logic at the inspected locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain product metadata, package integrity data, or registry entries. They do not contain executable crypto or network control logic.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Hits Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command findings occur in README and docs code fences that explain how to use the CLI. These are not hidden execution paths inside the package runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation snippets or product requirements text. Runtime command execution is separately covered in the medium findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Git Clone Followed by Agent File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The tool downloads remote skill content with git operations and writes transformed content into target editor paths. This is useful but exposes users to third-party skill supply-chain risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The clone and write paths are explicit. The risk depends on the trustworthiness of registry entries and selected remote repositories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Dev Panel HTML Injection Surface
dangerous_patterns.description: The management UI uses innerHTML with registry-derived values, creating an injection surface in local development mode.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sink is direct and receives category and skill fields. The affected feature is local developer tooling.

content.user_title: Install AI Agent Skills Across Editors
content.value_statement: Teams often manage AI agent skills separately for each editor, which creates drift and manual setup work. open-skills provides one CLI to browse, install, sync, and maintain skill sets across common AI coding tools.
actual_capabilities: Starts an interactive CLI flow for selecting target editors, install scope, categories, and skills.
Installs skill content into Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, Roo-Cline, Hermes, Antigravity, and GitHub Copilot paths.
Reads registry metadata from registry/skills.json and converts it into legacy category groups for installation.
Fetches GitHub and SkillStore metadata for discovery and remote skill resolution.
Creates local skill scaffolds and updates registry entries for development workflows.
Exports and imports installed skill stacks for repeatable setup across environments.
limitations: Remote skill installation depends on the trustworthiness of registry entries and source repositories.
SkillStore origins are resolved for metadata, but legacy installation may require usable Git source details.
The developer web panel is intended for local development and does not include authentication.
Directory cleanup can affect existing files in managed editor skill folders.

use_cases.title: Standardize team AI editor setup
use_cases.description: Install the same approved skill set across Claude Code, Codex-related workflows, Cursor, and other supported editors.

use_cases.title: Build and register local skills
use_cases.description: Create a local SKILL.md scaffold, add metadata, validate the registry, and prepare skills for distribution.

use_cases.title: Discover remote skill sources
use_cases.description: Search local registry entries and resolve GitHub or SkillStore metadata before adding new skills to a curated collection.

prompt_templates.title: Install selected skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Use open-skills to install frontend skills for Claude Code and Cursor in my local project. Show the choices before writing files.

prompt_templates.title: Create a new local skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new open-skills bundle named api-review for backend development. Add a short description and register it in the local registry.

prompt_templates.title: Audit installed skill stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Export my current open-skills stack, explain which editors have managed skills, and identify entries that need review before sharing.

prompt_templates.title: Curate remote registry entries
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for remote AI coding skills related to security review, compare their metadata, and prepare safe registry additions without installing them yet.

output_examples.input: Install frontend skills for Claude Code and Cursor.
output_examples.output: A guided selection summary with target editors, install scope, selected frontend skills, and final success or failure messages.

output_examples.input: Create a new backend API review skill.
output_examples.output: A new local skill folder, a starter SKILL.md file, and a registry entry with name, category, author, version, and license fields.
A stack summary listing each supported editor, scope, and recognized installed skills for repeatable setup.

best_practices: Review remote skill content before installing it into global editor paths.
Use local scope first when testing new skills or registry changes.
Back up existing editor rule and skill directories before running sync or update commands.
anti_patterns: Do not install unreviewed third-party skills directly into global AI agent instruction folders.
Do not run the developer web panel on a shared or exposed host.
Do not treat registry metadata as proof that a remote skill source is safe.

faq.question: Which editors does open-skills target?
faq.answer: It includes presets for Claude Code, Hermes, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, Cursor Skills, Roo-Cline, Antigravity, and GitHub Copilot.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The repository also includes editor presets used by related AI coding tools.

faq.question: Can it install remote skills?
faq.answer: Yes. It can clone GitHub or Git sources and can resolve metadata from GitHub and SkillStore.

faq.question: Is it safe to install any listed skill?
faq.answer: No. Skills are agent instructions, so users should review source content and trust the publisher before installation.

faq.question: What is the developer panel for?
faq.answer: The developer panel manages registry data during local development. It is not designed as an authenticated production service.

faq.question: Can it share an installed setup with another machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The export and import commands create and apply stack information for recognized installed skills.
